title: High-Production Systems & Seed Saving
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

competence_markers.description: The student manages a productive garden over multiple seasons with documented yields showing year-over-year improvement
The student has saved seed from at least five open-pollinated crops with proper technique
The student can diagnose nutrient deficiencies, pest damage, and disease symptoms from observation
The student can calculate garden production economics and identify high-value and low-value crops
The student has designed a nutrient cycling plan that reduces external inputs
The student can design and deliver basic garden instruction to beginners
The student maintains comprehensive records: planting dates, yields, pest observations, soil amendments, and economic analysis

content.why_this_matters:
You can plan, plant, manage, and harvest a productive garden. Now you learn to think at production scale: maximizing yield per square foot, closing nutrient loops, saving your own seed, diagnosing complex plant problems, managing protected growing environments, and teaching others to grow food.

This is the tier where gardening becomes a genuine food production skill — one that could feed a household, supply a community, or become the basis of a small agricultural enterprise. The student who completes this packet is not a hobbyist. They are a competent food producer who can grow meaningful quantities of food, reduce dependence on purchased inputs through nutrient cycling and seed saving, and troubleshoot the full range of problems a growing season presents.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Market garden productivity thinking** — yield per square foot, high-value crop selection, production economics
2. **Nutrient cycling at scale** — designing closed-loop fertility systems using compost, manure, and green manures
3. **Seed saving** — saving seed from open-pollinated varieties with proper isolation, selection, and storage
4. **Advanced plant problem diagnosis** — identifying nutrient deficiencies, diseases, and pest damage from observation
5. **Greenhouse/hoophouse management** — ventilation, humidity, winter production, economics of protected growing
6. **Teaching garden skills** — designing and delivering instruction for beginners
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Market Garden Productivity

The difference between a garden that supplements meals and a garden that meaningfully feeds a household is productivity per square foot. The market garden approach treats the garden as a production system, not a hobby space.

**High-value, high-yield crops for limited space:** Salad greens (lettuce, spinach, arugula, mesclun), herbs (basil, cilantro, parsley), tomatoes, peppers, and green beans return the most food value per square foot. Low-value, space-hungry crops — corn, pumpkins, watermelons — are not worth growing unless you have abundant space.

**The productivity calculation:** How much does your garden produce per square foot per year? A well-managed intensive garden produces $5–15 worth of food per square foot annually. A casual garden produces $1–3. The difference is succession planting, inter-planting, and season extension — all skills from Weathering Storms, now optimized.

**The economics question:** Calculate the real cost of your garden production. Include seeds, amendments, water, equipment amortization, and your labor at a reasonable hourly rate. Compare to the cost of equivalent produce at the grocery store or farmers market. Some crops pencil out beautifully (herbs, salad greens, tomatoes). Others are a net economic loss (potatoes in most climates, corn in small spaces). Grow what makes economic sense and purchase what does not — unless food sovereignty goals override economics.

### Part 2: Nutrient Cycling at Scale

A production garden that requires purchased fertilizer every year is an extractive system. The regenerative goal is to close as many nutrient loops as possible.

**The closed-loop model:**
- All crop residues are composted, not discarded
- All kitchen scraps return to the compost
- Animal manure from 2.5.4–2.5.6 is composted and applied
- Cover crops are grown in every bed during fallow periods
- Green manures (cover crops grown specifically for incorporation as fertilizer) supply nitrogen and organic matter

**Reducing purchased inputs:** Track what you buy. Lime, rock phosphate, greensand, seed — these are your external inputs. Each year, the goal is to reduce the list. A mature system may only need occasional mineral amendments and seed purchases, with all fertility generated internally.

### Part 3: Seed Saving

Saving seed is both a practical skill — seed independence — and a civilizational act. Every open-pollinated variety in existence was maintained by farmers who saved seed and selected for the traits they valued. When you save seed, you join a chain of human knowledge transfer that is older than writing.

**Open-pollinated versus hybrid:** Only open-pollinated (OP) varieties breed true from saved seed. Hybrid (F1) varieties produce seed, but the offspring will be variable and often inferior. Save seed from OP varieties only.

**Self-pollinating versus cross-pollinating crops:**
- **Self-pollinating** (tomatoes, peppers, beans, peas, lettuce): These crops pollinate themselves before the flower opens. Isolation is easy — different varieties can grow near each other with minimal crossing. Seed saving is straightforward.
- **Cross-pollinating** (squash, corn, brassicas, beets, carrots): These crops are pollinated by wind or insects and cross readily with other varieties of the same species. Saving seed requires isolation distances (1/2 mile for corn, 1/4 mile for squash in the same species) or hand pollination and bagging.

**Selection criteria:** Save seed from your best plants, not just the most convenient ones. Select for the traits that matter: vigor, disease resistance, productivity, flavor, earliness, or whatever your goals are. The farmer who saves seed from the first tomato to ripen selects for earliness. The farmer who saves from the heaviest producer selects for yield. This is applied genetics — slow, powerful, and cumulative over generations.

**Seed processing and storage:** Most vegetable seeds require fermentation (tomatoes, cucumbers), drying (beans, peas, lettuce), or threshing (grains, brassicas). Properly dried seed (below 8% moisture) stored in airtight containers in a cool, dark location retains viability for years. A jar of home-saved seed in the freezer represents food security that no store purchase can match.

Save seed from at least five crops during this packet.

### Part 4: Troubleshooting Plant Problems

The advanced grower diagnoses problems from observation, connecting visual symptoms to root causes.

**Nutrient deficiency patterns:**
- Nitrogen deficiency: older (lower) leaves yellow first, uniformly. Plant is pale, stunted, and unproductive.
- Phosphorus deficiency: leaves develop purple or reddish coloration, especially on undersides. Stunted growth. Common in cold soil.
- Potassium deficiency: leaf margins brown and curl (leaf scorch), starting on older leaves.
- Calcium deficiency: blossom end rot on tomatoes and peppers. New growth distorted.
- Iron deficiency: young leaves turn yellow between veins (interveinal chlorosis) while veins stay green. Common at high pH.
- Magnesium deficiency: older leaves show interveinal chlorosis. Similar to iron but on older, not younger, leaves.

**Disease versus pest versus nutrient:** The diagnostic challenge is distinguishing between these three causes when symptoms overlap. Yellowing leaves could be nitrogen deficiency, root rot, overwatering, or aphid feeding. Systematic elimination — checking soil moisture, inspecting roots, examining for pests, and reviewing soil test results — narrows the diagnosis.

### Part 5: Greenhouse and Hoophouse Management

Protected growing environments extend the season further than cold frames or row covers, but they introduce management challenges that open-air gardens do not face.

**Ventilation is the primary challenge.** An enclosed structure heats rapidly in sun and traps humidity. Overheating kills faster than frost. Humidity promotes fungal disease (botrytis, powdery mildew, damping off). The manager must open vents or roll up sides early on sunny days and close them before evening temperatures drop. Automated vent openers (thermal actuators) are worth the investment.

**Winter production under cover:** With no supplemental heat, an unheated hoophouse in zone 5–6 can produce salad greens, spinach, carrots, and hardy herbs from November through March. The key is planting in early fall so crops reach near-maturity before short days slow growth. The hoophouse then functions as storage — keeping harvested-quality crops alive through winter rather than actively growing them.

**Economics:** A basic hoophouse (14x48 feet) costs $2,000–5,000 depending on construction method. At market garden production rates, it can produce $3,000–8,000 worth of winter greens annually. Payback in 1–3 years is realistic for a market garden. For a household garden, the investment is justified by the value of year-round fresh production — but only if the structure is actively managed.

### Part 6: Teaching Garden Skills

Design and deliver a garden lesson or workshop for beginners — younger students, family members, or community members. Cover garden planning basics, seed starting, and at least one hands-on activity (planting, transplanting, or seed saving). Assess whether the participants learned something they can apply.

Help establish a garden for a family or individual with no prior experience. Walk them through site selection, bed preparation, crop selection, and a first planting. Follow up to ensure the garden produces food.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Production Documentation
**Developing well:** Comprehensive yield records by crop, economic analysis with real numbers, year-over-year comparison showing improvement. The student can explain what changes produced what results.

**Developing slowly:** Sporadic records. Vague claims of improvement without data.

### 2. Seed Saving Quality
**Developing well:** Seed saved from at least five crops with documented variety, selection criteria, isolation or pollination control, processing method, and storage. Germination test conducted before next planting.

**Developing slowly:** Seed saved casually without attention to isolation, selection, or storage. Likely to produce disappointing results.

### 3. Diagnostic Ability
**Developing well:** The student can walk through a garden and identify multiple problems from observation, proposing root-cause interventions rather than symptom treatments.

**Developing slowly:** The student identifies symptoms but defaults to generic responses ("needs fertilizer," "has a disease") without specific diagnosis.
